1: The address shifts from Moses alone to kol adat benei Yisrael — the entire congregation of the people of Israel. This is one of the few times in Leviticus that the audience is the full assembly rather than just Moses or the priests. The Holiness Code (chs. 17-26) addresses all Israel as a priestly people; holiness is not the exclusive concern of the Levitical caste but the calling of the entire covenant community.
2: Qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH eloheichem: you shall be holy, for I YHWH your God am holy. The theological foundation of the entire Holiness Code. Holiness is grounded in divine nature, not in human achievement: Israel is to be holy because YHWH is holy. The imitation-of-God ethic (imitatio Dei) that underlies Leviticus 19 — treating others as YHWH treats Israel — is one of the most sophisticated ethical frameworks in the ancient world. The declaration precedes any specific command: the imperative arises from the indicative of divine character.
3: Ish imo veavio tirau: each person shall fear (yira) his mother and his father. The reversal of the usual order (father-mother) is deliberate: mother is listed first, perhaps to balance the patriarchal cultural default. Yira (reverence, fear) rather than ahav (love) is used for parents — parental reverence encompasses respect, obedience, and maintenance. The conjunction of parent-fear and Sabbath-keeping in the same verse links domestic ethics and liturgical practice as equally important expressions of holiness.
4: Al tifnu el-haelilim: do not turn to idols (elilim = worthless nothings). The prohibition on idols and cast images at the opening of the practical holiness commands mirrors the Decalogue. The Holiness Code presupposes the Decalogue framework: its ethical content is the Decalogue applied across all domains of social life.
5: Vechi tizbechu zevach shelamim l-YHWH: when you sacrifice a peace offering (shelamim) to YHWH. The peace offering regulations here (vv. 5-8) parallel those of ch. 7: the offering must be eaten within two days; eating on the third day disqualifies the offering as piggul (pigul = desecration). The time-limit preserves the integrity of the communion meal: the sacred food must be consumed while still fresh, not stored as private provision. The two-day rule makes the peace offering a genuinely communal, time-bounded act of worship.
6: Beyom zivchachem ye'achel uvamachorat: it shall be eaten on the day of sacrifice and on the next day. The two-day eating window for the peace offering reflects both practical food-safety concerns (in a pre-refrigeration culture) and theological integrity: the meal is an act of worship, not a stockpile. The parallel in 7:15-18 establishes this as standard Levitical law.
7: Ve-im he'achol ye'achel bayom hashlishi piggul hu: if it is eaten at all on the third day, it is piggul (desecrated/loathsome). Piggul (an Akkadian loanword suggesting something offensive or stinking) describes food that has become ritually unfit — not by external contamination but by improper time of consumption. The third-day disqualification renders the entire offering retroactively invalid and the eater bears guilt (avon).
8: Veoklav avono yissa ki et-qodesh YHWH chillel: the one who eats it shall bear his iniquity, for he has profaned the holy thing of YHWH. Chillel (to profane, make common) is the opposite of qiddesh (to sanctify, make holy) — the same root as chol (ordinary, secular). Treating sacred food as ordinary by eating it too late collapses the distinction between holy and common that is fundamental to Levitical theology.
9: Uvekutzrachem et-qetzir artzechem lo techaleh peat sadecha liqtzor: when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap to the edge (peat) of your field. The gleaning laws (vv. 9-10) are the Holiness Code's most direct expression of economic ethics. Peat (corner, edge) and laqat (to glean, pick up fallen grain) define the portions intentionally left for the poor. Agricultural abundance comes with a built-in redistribution mechanism: the edges of the field and the dropped grain belong to the poor and the stranger by divine command. Ruth 2 is the narrative embodiment of this law.
10: Vecharmelcha lo teolel uferaten karmecha lo telaqqet laani velagen tetov otam: you shall not strip your vineyard bare (teolel = to glean thoroughly, pick over), and you shall not gather the fallen grapes (peret) — leave them for the poor and the sojourner. The vineyard parallel to the grain-field: both have an intentional margin of incompletion. The gleaning laws institutionalize generosity by making it a matter of divine command rather than voluntary charity. The poor have a right to the margins; the owner must actively restrain from full extraction.
11: Lo tignovu velo techachedu velo teshaqru ish bamito: you shall not steal (ganav), deal falsely (kachash), or lie to one another. The triplet of theft, deception, and lying covers the main modes of dishonest social interaction. Kachash (to deny, deal falsely, deceive by understatement) and shaqar (to lie) together cover both implicit and explicit dishonesty. The specific construction ish bamito (a man toward his fellow/companion) places these prohibitions in the context of neighbor-relationships — the same context as the love-neighbor command of v18.
12: Velo tishavu bishmi lashaqer: you shall not swear falsely by my name. The false oath invokes the divine name to guarantee a lie — a double violation: dishonesty plus misuse of the divine name (cf. the third commandment, Exod 20:7). Challel et shem elohecha (you would profane the name of your God) — false oaths are not merely contractual fraud but theological profanation. The name of YHWH cannot be used as an instrument of deception without damaging the relationship between the community and its God.
13: Lo taashoq et-reecha velo tigzol: you shall not oppress (ashaq) your neighbor or rob (gazal) him. Ashaq (to oppress, exploit, extort) covers economic coercion — using power to extract from those who are vulnerable. The wages of a hired worker must not be held overnight — lo-talin peulat sachir itecha ad-boqer. The same day-wage rule appears in Deut 24:15. ANE labor contracts often included provisions about timely payment, but here it is a divine command. The vulnerability of the day laborer — who depends on same-day wages for that evening's food — means delayed payment is effectively theft.
14: Lo teqallel cheresh velifnei iver lo titen michshol: you shall not curse the deaf (cheresh), and before the blind (iver) you shall not put a stumbling block. The deaf person cannot hear the curse; the blind cannot see the obstacle placed before them — they are by definition unable to defend themselves. The prohibition of exploiting vulnerability is extended to situations where the victim cannot even perceive the offense. Veyarerta mielohecha: you shall fear (yare) your God — the divine omniscience supplies what human accountability lacks; YHWH sees even what the victim cannot.
15: Lo ta'asu avel bamishpat: you shall not do injustice (avel) in judgment (mishpat). The prohibition of partiality in legal proceedings — not favoring the poor (dal) or being partial to the great (gadol). The symmetric prohibition against both economic-class favoritism (favoring the poor against the rich) and power-class favoritism (favoring the wealthy/influential) establishes that justice is blind to social status. Betzedek tishpot amitecha: in righteousness you shall judge your fellow. The positive formulation completes the negative: not just avoiding injustice but actively practicing righteousness.
16: Lo telech rachil baamecha: you shall not go about (halak) as a slanderer (rachil) among your people. Rachil (slanderer, gossip, from the same root as merchant/trader) suggests someone who traffics in damaging information about others. Lo taamod al dam reecha: you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor — failing to act when someone's life is at stake. The juxtaposition of slander (active harm through speech) with failure to rescue (passive harm through silence) covers both the misuse and the withholding of speech in neighbor relationships.
17: Lo tisna et-achicha bilvavecha: you shall not hate your brother in your heart. The internalization of the ethical command is significant: the prohibition extends beyond external action to interior attitude. Bilvavecha (in your heart) makes hidden contempt as prohibited as open hostility. Hocheach tochiach et-amitecha: you shall certainly rebuke (hocheach tochiach, infinitive absolute for emphasis) your neighbor. Active rebuke is commanded as the alternative to silent resentment — confronting a wrong is more loving than nursing a grievance. Velo tisa alav chet: you shall not bear sin because of him — failing to rebuke makes the bystander complicit in the sin.
18: Lo tiqom velo-titor et-benei ammecha veahavta lereiacha kamocha ani YHWH: you shall not take vengeance or bear a grudge (nator = to keep, maintain, preserve — a grudge is a grievance preserved) against the sons of your people, but you shall love (ahav) your neighbor (rea) as yourself. The command to love neighbor is the climax of the preceding sequence (vv. 11-17). Rea (neighbor, fellow, companion) in context covers the full range of social relationships within the community. The self-referential standard (kamocha = as yourself) grounds the command in universal human self-interest: the measure of what you owe your neighbor is what you know you need yourself. The closing ani YHWH (I am YHWH) supplies both authority and motivation: the covenant God who loves Israel commands Israel to extend that love laterally.
19: Huqqotai tishmorunu: you shall keep my statutes. The section on forbidden mixtures (kilayim) addresses: (1) breeding livestock with different species; (2) sowing a field with two kinds of seed (sha'atnez applies here); (3) wearing a garment woven from two materials (sha'atnez = mixed fiber cloth, wool-and-linen). The kilayim (mixtures, from kele = to restrain, hold back) prohibitions preserve divinely established categories. The same principle underlying the clean/unclean distinctions — categories must not be collapsed — governs the kilayim rules: the integrity of created kinds reflects the integrity of the Creator's order.
20: Ve-ish ki yishkav et-isha shikhvat-zera: when a man lies with a woman and there is intercourse. This case involves a slave woman betrothed to another man but not yet redeemed or granted freedom. The case sits between fully free and fully slave, creating a liminal legal status that requires a specific ruling. Because she is not fully free, the death penalty for adultery does not apply; instead a biqqoret (investigation, inquiry, or scourging — the term is debated) is required, and a guilt offering (asham) is brought.
21: Vehevi et-ashamo l-YHWH: he shall bring his guilt offering (asham) to YHWH. The guilt offering in this case provides atonement for the violation of the betrothed slave woman's protected status. The requirement of a formal atonement ritual for what might otherwise be treated as a purely civil matter demonstrates that sexual ethics in the Holiness Code has a theological dimension: violations against persons are violations against YHWH's covenant order.
22: Vechipper alav hakkohen biil ha-asham lifnei YHWH al-chatato asher chata venislach lo me-chatato asher chata: the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH, and he shall be forgiven for the sin he has committed. The atonement-and-forgiveness formula here — identical to the standard sin/guilt offering formula — applies even in the slave-woman case. No violation is beyond the reach of the atonement system, but none is beneath requiring it either.
23: Veki tavo-u el-haaretz untemtem kol-etz maakal vaarltem orlato et-piryo: when you enter the land and plant any fruit tree, you shall treat its fruit as forbidden (orlah, literally: uncircumcised). The first three years of fruit are arel (uncircumcised, forbidden) — not to be eaten. Orlah (the foreskin; used metaphorically for what is sealed/restricted) is applied to fruit, connecting the circumcision covenant-mark to agricultural practice. The first-fruits theology requires patient waiting: the tree must establish itself before humans take its fruit. Mishnah Orlah is an entire tractate devoted to the practical application of this law.
24: Uvhashanah hareviyit yihyeh kol-piryo qodesh hillulim l-YHWH: in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy — a jubilation offering (hillulim, from halel = to praise) to YHWH. The fourth-year fruit is entirely dedicated to YHWH as a praise offering — hillulim is a festive, joyful offering. Only in the fifth year may the owner eat the fruit for personal benefit. The delayed-gratification structure (3 years forbidden + 1 year dedicated + 5th year yours) teaches that the land's produce belongs to YHWH first.
25: Uvhashanah hachamishit tochelu et-piryo lehosif lachem tevuatah: in the fifth year you may eat its fruit, to increase its yield for you — I am YHWH your God. The obedience to the orlah laws increases the tree's yield (lehosif... tevuatah): the theological principle that covenant obedience produces abundance is applied even to horticultural practice. The closing ani YHWH eloheichem connects the agricultural law to covenant identity.
26: Lo tochelu al-hadam: you shall not eat any flesh with the blood (al-hadam). The prohibition on blood-eating echoes Lev 17:10-14 and connects to the ANE divination practice of eating flesh with blood in magical/cultic contexts. The following prohibitions on divination (nachash) and augury (anan) are in the same cluster — the blood-eating, divination, and augury prohibitions address practices that sought to access spiritual power outside the covenant framework.
27: Lo taqifu peat roshechem velo tashchit et-peat zeqanecha: you shall not round off the hair on your temples (peat rosh) or mar the edges of your beard. The hair-cutting prohibition addresses mourning practices common in Canaanite religion — shaving specific areas of the head and beard was associated with pagan rituals for the dead. The prohibition distinguishes Israelite mourning from syncretistic practices (cf. Deut 14:1). The peat rosh regulations may also be connected to clan-identification markings.
28: Veseret lanefesh lo titenu bivesarchem uchtovt qaqqa lo titenu bachem: you shall not make any cuts (seret) on your body for the dead, or tattoo yourselves (ketovot qa'aqa) — I am YHWH. Both cutting for the dead (a mourning rite common in Baal worship; cf. 1 Kgs 18:28) and tattooing (permanent body marking) are prohibited as pagan practices that mark the body as belonging to another deity or that participate in death-cult rituals. Ani YHWH closes the prohibition: the body belongs to YHWH, not to the death-powers of surrounding religion.
29: Al techallel et-bitcha lehaznotah: do not degrade your daughter by making her a prostitute (zanah). The warning against cult prostitution (or economic exploitation of daughters through prostitution) — velo-tizne haaretz umalah haaretz zimmah (lest the land become full of zimmah, a term for sexual wickedness/shameful scheming). The land itself is conceived as a moral participant: sexual sin fills the land with corruption in the same way that blood guilt does (Num 35:33).
30: Et-shabbetotai tishmoru umiqdashi tirau: you shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary — the refrain that brackets the Holiness Code (cf. 26:2). The Sabbath and sanctuary commands appear again here in the middle of the chapter's ethical content, reminding that the social ethics of ch. 19 cannot be separated from the liturgical framework: ethical behavior and worship belong together.
31: Al tifnu el-haoboth veal haydeonim al-tevaqshu letomah bahem: do not turn to mediums (ovoth) or wizards/necromancers (yideoni). Ov (a medium, one who consults the dead — possibly referring to the pit-spirit through which necromancers communicated) and yideoni (a knowing one, a familiar spirit) cover the main forms of occult consultation in the ANE. The prohibition is grounded positively in covenant relationship: seeking knowledge from the dead is a rejection of YHWH as the source of guidance and knowledge.
32: Mipnei seivah taqum vehadarta penei zaqen veyarata meelohecha: you shall stand (taqum) before the gray-haired and honor the face of the aged, and you shall fear your God. The elderly are owed honor not because of individual achievement but because of age itself — a principle that cuts against both meritocracy and youth-culture. The connection between honoring elders and fearing God (veyarata meelohecha) grounds intergenerational respect in the same theological place as respect for the vulnerable: YHWH sees what human status-systems ignore.
33: Vechi yagur itecha ger beartzechem lo tonub: when a stranger (ger) sojourns with you in your land, you shall not wrong (onah) him. Ger (sojourner, resident alien — not a temporary visitor but a long-term resident without native-born rights) receives specific legal protection in the Holiness Code. Onah (to wrong, oppress, afflict — the same root as ana in 19:13) covers economic, social, and legal exploitation of resident foreigners.
34: Keozerach mikem yihyeh lachem hager hagor itchem veahavta lo kamocha ki gerim heyitem beeretz mitzraim: the stranger who sojourns with you shall be to you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt. The extension of the love-neighbor command (v18) to the ger is among the most remarkable commands in Leviticus. The motivation is empathetic-historical: Israel's own experience as foreigners (gerim) in Egypt is the ground for treating foreigners well. The Exodus memory is the ethical engine that drives neighbor-extension.
35: Lo ta'asu avel bamishpat bammidah bammishqal uvammeshura: you shall not do injustice in judgment, in measures of length (midah), in weight (mishqal), or in quantity (meshurah). The prohibition on false weights and measures covers three dimensions of commercial measurement. The ANE merchant carried two sets of weights — heavier for buying, lighter for selling — a practice explicitly condemned here and in Amos 8:5, Micah 6:11, Proverbs 11:1.
36: Mozanei tzedeq avnei tzedeq eifat tzedeq vehin tzedeq yihyeh lachem: just balances (mozanei tzedeq), just weights (avnei tzedeq), a just ephah (eifat tzedeq), and a just hin (hin tzedeq) you shall have. Tzedeq (righteousness, justice, right) applied to commercial instruments: the scales, stones, and measures must themselves be characterized by justice. The context is the Exodus: ani YHWH eloheichem asher hotzeiti etchem meeretz mitzraim (I am YHWH your God who brought you out of Egypt) — the God who redeemed Israel with exact precision demands exact precision in commercial dealings.
37: Ushmartem et kol-chuqotai veet-kol-mishpatai vaasitem otam ani YHWH: you shall observe all my statutes and all my rules and do them — I am YHWH. The closing verse of the Holiness Code's practical ethics section provides the same double formula (keep + do) that opens ch. 19 (v3: tishmoru... veshabbetotai). The bracket of Sabbath/sanctuary at vv. 3 and 30, combined with the opening (v2) and closing (v37) holiness-formula, gives ch. 19 a carefully structured literary shape around the central love-neighbor command of v18.
